cards.definition: the art of capturing moving images on film — includes camera shots, angles, movement, and lighting
all visual elements placed before the camera — setting, costume, lighting, performance, and composition
assembling film shots into a sequence — creates pace, rhythm, meaning, and narrative continuity
all sound elements in a film — dialogue, sound effects, foley, and music (score and soundtrack)
the typical features audiences expect from a genre — e.g. horror uses dark lighting, jump scares, isolated settings
the idea that a film director is the primary creative force behind a film — their recurring themes and style are the 'signature' of the work
the shape of a film's story — often follows Todorov's equilibrium model: equilibrium → disruption → new equilibrium
a model suggesting stories follow a pattern: initial equilibrium, disruption, recognition, repair, new equilibrium
Vladimir Propp identified recurring character types in stories — hero, villain, helper, princess, false hero etc.
how groups of people and social issues are portrayed — films can reinforce or challenge stereotypes and ideologies
high-budget films produced by major studios for mass global audiences — uses stars, high production values, and generic formulas
films produced outside the major studio system with lower budgets — often more experimental or socially conscious
films produced in the UK — often address specifically British social issues and have a distinct cultural identity
getting a film to audiences — through cinema release, streaming, DVD, or broadcast. Studios spend heavily on marketing
a classification indicating the suitability of a film for different ages — in the UK set by the BBFC (U, PG, 12A, 15, 18)
the circumstances in which a film was made and received — understanding context helps explain the film's themes and messages
Eduqas specifies films for study — typically including one Hollywood film, one British film, and one global film from outside the English-speaking world
films produced outside Hollywood and the English-speaking world — Eduqas requires study of at least one global film
a non-fiction film representing reality — can be observational, expository, or participatory in style
groups of filmmakers sharing a style or philosophy — e.g. French New Wave, Italian Neorealism, British Kitchen Sink realism
